burial.plot_notes: Forest Home Cemetery Milwaukee WI per FA

career.organization: Kellogg & Strong Steamboat Company and Warehouse, Milwaukee

narrative_blocks.header: Helped secure the land for Forest Home Cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Hunter Strong was instrumental in securing the land for Forest Home Cemetery in Milwaukee and persuading St. Paul's Episcopal Church to establish the cemetery there — a civic contribution that anchored Milwaukee's most prominent burial ground. He came to Wisconsin around 1830, clerked in the territorial legislature in 1839-40, built his fortune through the Kellogg & Strong Steamboat company and Newberry Furnace, and was twice married.

narrative_blocks.header: Bridged two Strong-Hooker marriages and two frontier states
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Hunter Strong was the son of the second Strong-Hooker marriage — Judge Elisha Beebe Strong and Dolly Goodwin Hooker — and the father of the man who would carry that name to a third generation: Beebe Hooker Strong, born Milwaukee 1866. He came to Wisconsin around 1830 at age ten, making the journey from the Rochester land grants his father had built to the Great Lakes frontier, and with him the Strong-Hooker legacy crossed into a new American West.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
History of Descendants of Elder John Strong, Vol. 1 (1871)

burial.plot_notes: Forest Home Cemetery Milwaukee per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her Milwaukee pioneer husband by nineteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Rosa Searls Warner Strong, widowed when Robert Hunter Strong died at Waukesha in May 1892, lived on for nineteen more years — dying in Chicago on July 14, 1911 at age 87 while visiting her daughter, having outlasted her husband long enough to see her children established across three states. Her obituary noted that Robert had been instrumental in founding Forest Home Cemetery and that the family had been prominent at St. Paul's Episcopal Church — the church whose cemetery he helped build would ultimately receive them both.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester NY per FA

notable.notable_blurb: First Judge of Monroe County, NY; Yale 1809

education.notes: Prepared for college by Nathan Johnson. Yale class of 1809.
Attended Litchfield Law School 1810; thereafter read law in Canandaigua NY.

career.notes: Partner with William H. Adams. Admitted to bar 1812.
Purchased 1,000 acres near Rochester with Elisha Beach; developed Carthage area; erected storehouses, incline railway, bridge over lower falls.
Appointed March 5, 1821 by Governor DeWitt Clinton. Monroe County organized February 23, 1821.
Moved to Detroit 1861; died there Oct 14, 1867.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended 1810; appointed first judge of Monroe County NY by 1821.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Strong men who married Hooker descendants, 84 years apart

narrative_blocks.header: First Judge of Monroe County, appointed 1821
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Beebe Strong was appointed the first judge of Monroe County, New York on March 5, 1821 by Governor DeWitt Clinton — just days after the county itself was organized by the state legislature from Ontario County territory. A Yale graduate (1809) and Litchfield Law School alumnus, he had also served in the New York State Assembly in 1819-1820 and as a presidential elector in 1824, becoming one of the most prominent legal figures in upstate New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His Hooker marriage repeated a Strong family tradition
narrative_blocks.body: When Elisha Beebe Strong married Dolly Goodwin Hooker in 1813 at Canandaigua, New York, he was not the first Strong to wed a Thomas Hooker descendant: 84 years earlier, his ancestral kin Captain Asahel Strong Jr. had married Sarah Hooker of Farmington, Connecticut in 1729, producing a line that named a grandson 'Asahel Hooker Strong' to preserve the memory. The two unions — Farmington 1729, Canandaigua 1813 — trace the same Strong-Hooker affinity across the Great Migration and the early Republic, from a Farmington lawyer's household to a frontier judge's courtroom on the Erie Canal.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker women who married Strongs in 1729 and 1813

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband was Monroe County's first judge
narrative_blocks.body: Dolly Goodwin Hooker married Elisha Beebe Strong, who in 1821 became the first judge of Monroe County, New York. She died in Rochester in 1850; he remarried and moved to Detroit, dying there in 1867.

narrative_blocks.header: Her Hooker-Strong marriage echoed one from 1729
narrative_blocks.body: When Dolly Goodwin Hooker married Elisha Beebe Strong in 1813, she entered a family that had already wed into the Hooker line 84 years before: Captain Asahel Strong Jr. had married her distant cousin Sarah Hooker at Farmington in 1729, and a grandson of that marriage — Asahel Hooker Strong — was already carrying her maiden name as his own middle name by 1776. Dolly's marriage completed the second arc; her great-grandson Beebe Hooker Strong, born in Milwaukee in 1866, would carry the name to a third generation on the Wisconsin frontier.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester NY per FA
